planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; in zone 9, sow September through February for fall and winter production — the primary growing season runs October through April, with spring plants bolting in May when temperatures rise.

directions: Direct sow in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade during the cool season; Florida Broadleaf is the classic Southern mustard green — the most widely grown mustard green cultivar in the Gulf South for generations, with large, flat, broad, smooth, dark-green leaves with a pungent, spicy mustard flavor that is a cornerstone of traditional Southern cooking. Unlike curled mustard varieties (Southern Giant Curled, Green Wave) that have ruffled, frilly leaves, Florida Broadleaf has smooth, flat leaves that are easier to wash and which have a slightly more robust, bold flavor. Florida Broadleaf is grown in zone 9 gardens from September through April — the cool-season growing window when mustard greens develop the best flavor and avoid the extreme bitterness and quick bolting that heat causes. Young leaves are mild enough for salads; mature leaves are best cooked, typically braised with smoked meat (ham hock, smoked turkey) and seasoned vinegar in the traditional Southern style. Frost improves mustard green flavor — the conversion of starches to sugars after cold nights produces a richer, more complex, slightly sweeter flavor. Harvest outer leaves continuously or cut the entire plant at 4–6 inches above the crown for a cut-and-come-again harvest.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; in zone 9, sow September through January for fall and winter production; the striking purple foliage makes Red Giant ornamentally valuable in cool-season beds alongside kale and Swiss chard.

directions: Direct sow in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade during the cool season; Red Giant is the most widely grown purple mustard green — a Japanese mustard variety with large, deeply frilled to semi-smooth leaves in a striking deep burgundy-purple to reddish-purple color that makes it simultaneously ornamental and edible. The bold purple color intensifies with cool temperatures and frost exposure, making Red Giant one of the most visually dramatic cool-season vegetables for the Gulf South garden — combining beautifully with green kale, white cauliflower, and orange-flowered pansies in the fall edible landscape. The flavor is distinctly pungent and spicy with a mustard heat that develops more fully in mature leaves; young leaves at 4–6 inches are milder and excellent for salads and slaws. In hot weather, the plant bolts and the leaves become extremely pungent and bitter — in zone 9, grow strictly as a fall/winter/spring crop. The purple color partially transfers to cooking liquid and mixed dishes, giving cooked dishes a beautiful purple-tinted sauce. The combination of culinary value and ornamental purple foliage makes Red Giant an excellent choice for edible landscape plantings where vegetables serve double duty as ornamentals.

common_name: Joi Choi Pak Choi (Vigorous Upright Bok Choy)

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1/4 inch deep or transplant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade during cool seasons; Joi Choi bolt resistance is notably better than standard pak choi cultivars, extending the harvest window in warm spring conditions.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade during cool seasons; Joi Choi is the most widely recommended pak choi (bok choy) cultivar for American home gardens — an All-America Selections award winner producing an upright, well-formed head with crisp, white, thick, succulent stems and dark-green, smooth leaf blades in a clean, classic pak choi form. Joi Choi's excellence comes from the combination of very clean, upright growth habit (the stems don't spread outward messily like some varieties), exceptional bolt resistance for an early-maturing type (30–50 days), and excellent flavor — the stems are tender and mildly sweet, the leaves are tender with mild cabbage flavor, and the whole plant can be used in stir-fries, soups, and raw preparations. In zone 9, Joi Choi is grown September through April as a cool-season crop; in the heat of summer it bolts quickly. The bolt resistance is Joi Choi's key advantage — allowing it to be planted in late February and early March (early spring zone 9) before the heat arrives, with harvest extending a week or two longer than standard varieties. Harvest the whole head at soil level when it reaches full size (10–12 inches tall) or harvest outer leaves continuously for a prolonged cut-and-come-again harvest.

common_name: California Blackeye Pea (California Blackeye Southern Pea)

planting.note: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after soil is thoroughly warm (minimum 65°F); in zone 9, sow April through July for summer production — the long warm season allows 2–3 successive plantings for continuous harvest.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in lean to moderately fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after the soil is thoroughly warm; California Blackeye is the most commercially significant blackeye pea cultivar — the variety responsible for most of the blackeye peas sold in American grocery stores. The distinctive black-eyed, cream-colored dry beans cook quickly, have a mild, earthy, slightly sweet flavor, and a mealy texture that makes them a staple of Southern cooking — particularly the iconic Southern New Year's Day dish of hoppin' john (black-eyed peas and rice), eaten for good luck. California Blackeye is a semi-bush type that is more compact and less sprawling than old-fashioned vine types, making it manageable in typical garden beds without staking. The fresh green pods are eaten as snap beans or the fresh shelled green peas are eaten before the seeds dry; the dried seeds are the standard black-eyed peas of commerce. In zone 9, California Blackeye thrives in the long hot season, producing crops from June through September with successive plantings. Southern peas fix nitrogen in the soil through symbiotic bacteria and actually improve soil fertility — one of the few warm-season vegetables that enriches rather than depletes garden soil.

common_name: Heavy Metal Switchgrass (Steel Blue Upright Switchgrass)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Heavy Metal is adaptable to clay, wet, and dry soils — one of the most versatile native ornamental grasses for difficult sites.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Heavy Metal is the most celebrated upright switchgrass cultivar — named for its stiffly vertical, non-flopping growth habit and distinctive steel-blue summer foliage color, in contrast to the arching, floppy form of the switchgrass species and many other cultivars. The rigid, bolt-upright stems (4–5 feet tall) maintain their strict vertical form through all weather without flopping or spreading outward — a critical landscape quality that makes Heavy Metal outstanding for formal accent use, vertical punctuation in borders, and screening where a reliably upright column of foliage is desired. The summer foliage is an attractive steel-blue to blue-green color; in fall it transitions through yellow, orange, and burgundy for one of the finest fall color displays of any native grass. Pink seed heads wave in late summer breezes. Heavy Metal is a prairie native (Panicum virgatum) that is exceptionally adaptable — tolerating clay, periodic flooding, drought, and the full range of Gulf South conditions that challenge many ornamental grasses. The wildlife value is significant: the seeds are eaten by many songbirds, and switchgrass provides nesting habitat and overwintering cover for many native insects and small mammals. Cut to 4–6 inches in late winter before new growth emerges.
